Okay, so to give you kind of a quick breakdown of the article generation workflow, so what we did was we broke down like the writing.
Process into multiple steps.
I just generated like one article here.
I didn't provide any human input.
So it's like super like very like basic AI output.
But we start by providing a keyword or an assignment title and we give it an assignment direction.
And this time I just like told it to write a step by step guide explaining how like solopreneurs can build an MVP app.
At this stage, we will usually provide an outline that we edit pretty heavily.
So we will do like our own research on the top ranking content and like what we want to include.
And we will provide a custom outline here or at the very least a much longer assignment direction.
Because if we leave this blank, then the AI will default to a more like to a generic outline that's provided in atlases.
And we don't want to do that.
So we want to make sure that the outline and the blog content itself is optimized for AI and SEO.
So we will do that at this stage.
And then we will generate the assignment brief.
The assignment brief uses a SEMrush API to kind of connect to get all the SEO data, which includes a keyword analysis.
So it will combine a list of like the top ranking content.
It will analyze how long each blog is.
It's going to return like search volume, whether the search intent is informational or commercial.
In this case, it identified it was like an informational search intent.
And it also returns a list of other keyword opportunities.
So related to keywords that we should mention.
And then it generates a first version of the outline.
This proposed content.
Structure is very similar to the outline we provide in the assignment direction.
So if we say write a how-to guide, it's going to be a how-to guide.
If we say write a listicle, it's going to be a listicle.

And I didn't, like, make any changes here, but we will typically go in and, like, review this in detail, make sure it's providing the right level of detail, like the information is organized in a coherent way.
We just want to make sure that the structure is on point because the most, I think the most difficult editing happens when the structure is kind of all over the place.
So we just want to make sure it's following, like, a coherent line of thought and that all, like, the headings are building on top of each other and, like, following the thesis and the title.
Once we're happy with this, we'll move forward with the research step.
The research step is connected to different researcher APIs.
For example, one of them is per platform.

So what it does, it takes the assignment brief, and then it asks different questions about different sections in the assignment brief, and then it's going to compile answers to those questions, and it just compiles all of that into a research document that looks like this.

So it just provides enough information about a specific topic so that we know how we want to, so that the LLM knows how to present the information, and that it has enough information to reference for the blog post.
So right now, Bubble is very much, like, featured here, because I instructed that in the assignment direction to kind of focus on Bubble.
But if this was, like, a more generic blog post, it would be, like, combining, like, different research materials from different sources.
So...
We do not typically edit the research document.
We just proceed to the outline stage, which is where we will further refine the outline, make any changes as needed.
We can also kind of change the word counts, remove specific bullet points.
Once we're happy with the outline, we draft the content, we run the fact checker, and we also run the internal link generator.
We just, it adds internal links to the content to make it easier, you know, for indexing.

So the content manager will read and edit the blog.
